perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees that the Confidential Information will be kept confidential and that Recipient and its Representatives will not disclose any of the Confidential Information in any manner whatsoever; provided, however , that (i) it may make any disclosure of the Confidential Information to which the Disclosing Party gives its prior written consent, and (ii) any of the Confidential Information may be disclosed to Recipient’s Representatives who need to know such information for the sole purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction, who are bound by confidentiality obligations to Recipient or agree to keep such Confidential Information confidential to the same extent as Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees that the Confidential Information should generally be kept confidential and that Recipient and its Representatives will try not to disclose the Confidential Information in any manner whatsoever; provided, however , that (i) it may make any disclosure of the Confidential Information to which the Disclosing Party gives its prior written consent, and (ii) any of the Confidential Information may be disclosed to Recipient’s Representatives who seem to need to know such information for the purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction, who are bound by confidentiality obligations to Recipient or agree to keep such Confidential Information confidential to approximately the same extent as Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: The modification replaces "will be kept" with "should generally be kept," weakening the confidentiality obligation. Additionally, the phrase "try not to disclose" is vague, as opposed to the original binding language. Changing "who need to know" to "who seem to need to know" introduces subjectivity into the assessment of necessity. Finally, "to the same extent as Recipient" becomes "to approximately the same extent as Recipient", making the obligation imprecise.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text enforces a stricter, more enforceable standard of confidentiality, whereas the modification introduces ambiguity regarding the degree of confidentiality and the definition of 'need to know,' which undermines the reasonable expectation of confidentiality which can be considered an unfair trade practice if violated in Massachusetts under M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 11 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 11 Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 11: Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11. Any person who engages in the conduct of any trade or commerce and who suffers any loss of money or property, real or personal, as a result of the use or employment by another person who engages in any trade or commerce of an unfair method of competition or an unfair or deceptive act or practice declared unlawful by section two or by any rule or regulation issued under paragraph (c) of section two may, as hereinafter provided, bring an action in the superior court, or in the housing court as provided in section three of chapter one hundred and eighty-five C, whether by way of original complaint, counterclaim, cross-claim or third-party action for damages and such equitable relief, including an injunction, as the court deems to be necessary and proper. Such person, if he has not suffered any loss of money or property, may obtain such an injunction if it can be shown that the aforementioned unfair method of competition, act or practice may have the effect of causing such loss of money or property. Any persons entitled to bring such action may, if the use or employment of the unfair method of competition or the unfair or deceptive act or practice has caused similar injury to numerous other persons similarly situated and if the court finds in a preliminary hearing that he adequately and fairly represents such other persons, bring the action on behalf of himself and such other similarly injured and

Any person who engages in the conduct of any trade or commerce and who suffers any loss of money or property, real or personal, as a result of the use or employment by another person who engages in any trade or commerce of an unfair method of competition or an unfair or deceptive act or practice declared unlawful by section two or by any rule or regulation issued under paragraph (c) of section two may, as hereinafter provided, bring an action in the superior court, or in the housing court as provided in section three of chapter one hundred and eighty-five C, whether by way of original complaint, counterclaim, cross-claim or third-party action for damages and such equitable relief, including an injunction, as the court deems to be necessary and proper. Any persons entitled to bring such action may, if the use or employment of the unfair method of comp
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the confidentiality obligations, creating ambiguity. The original text states the Confidential Information "will be kept confidential," while the modified text uses "should generally be kept confidential," introducing uncertainty. Similarly, "will not disclose" becomes "try not to disclose," further weakening the obligation. This ambiguity undermines the reasonable expectation of confidentiality and could be considered an unfair trade practice under Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93A, Section 11, as it relates to unfair or deceptive acts in trade or commerce. Although the snippets do not mention confidentiality directly, they establish the legal basis for actions against unfair trade practices, which the weakening of a confidentiality clause could potentially fall under.

perturbation.original_text: In any event, Recipient agrees to undertake reasonable precautions to safeguard and protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information, to accept responsibility for any breach of this Agreement by any of its Representatives, and at its sole expense to take all reasonable measures to restrain its Representatives from prohibited or unauthorized disclosure or uses of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: In any event, Recipient will endeavor to take precautions to safeguard and protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information, and will consider addressing breaches of this Agreement by any of its Representatives, and will, if feasible, take some measures to restrain its Representatives from prohibited or unauthorized disclosure or uses of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications replace "agrees to undertake reasonable precautions" with "will endeavor to take precautions," lessening the obligation. "Accept responsibility" becomes "will consider addressing," making accountability discretionary. "Take all reasonable measures" is weakened to "will, if feasible, take some measures," making the duty to restrain representatives optional and significantly less stringent.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing inherent in Massachusetts contracts, which Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93A, Section 2 can be interpreted as applying to. By weakening the obligation to protect confidential information and making actions discretionary, the altered text allows for actions that could be considered unfair or deceptive, potentially violating the law. The change allows the recipient to not be responsible or at least, to consider whether to be responsible, which is contradictory to good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. Such rules and regulations shall not be inconsistent with the rules, regulations and decisions of the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts interpreting the provisions of 15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1) (The Federal Trade Commission Act), as from time to time amended. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates 'reasonable precautions,' 'accept[ing] responsibility,' and 'tak[ing] all reasonable measures.' The changed text weakens these obligations to 'endeavor to take precautions,' 'will consider addressing,' and 'will, if feasible, take some measures.' This violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, potentially constituting an unfair or deceptive practice under Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93A, Section 2, as this section declares unlawful 'unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices.' By weakening the obligations to protect confidential information, the altered text allows actions that could be seen as unfair, thereby contradicting the law.

perturbation.original_text: If, in the absence of a protective order or other remedy or the receipt of a waiver by the Disclosing Party, Recipient or any of its Representatives are nonetheless, in the written opinion of counsel, legally compelled to disclose Confidential Information or Discussion Information to any tribunal or else stand liable for contempt or suffer other censure or penalty, Recipient or its Representatives may, without liability hereunder, disclose to such tribunal only that portion of the Confidential Information or Discussion Information which such counsel advises is legally required to be disclosed, provided that Recipient exercises its commercially reasonable efforts to preserve the confidentiality of the Confidential Information and the Discussion Information, including, without limitation, by cooperating with the Disclosing Party to obtain an appropriate protective order or other reliable assurance that confidential treatment will be accorded the Confidential Information and the Discussion Information by such tribunal.
perturbation.changed_text: If, in the absence of a protective order or other remedy or the receipt of a waiver by the Disclosing Party, Recipient or any of its Representatives are nonetheless, in the written opinion of counsel, legally compelled to disclose Confidential Information or Discussion Information to any tribunal or else stand liable for contempt or suffer other censure or penalty, Recipient or its Representatives may, without liability hereunder, disclose to such tribunal that portion of the Confidential Information or Discussion Information which such counsel generally indicates is advisable to be disclosed, provided that Recipient considers whether to make efforts to preserve the confidentiality of the Confidential Information and the Discussion Information, perhaps by cooperating with the Disclosing Party to try to obtain a protective order.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes "legally required to be disclosed" to "generally indicates is advisable to be disclosed," replacing a legal standard with a subjective assessment. The change also replaces "Recipient exercises its commercially reasonable efforts to preserve the confidentiality" to "Recipient considers whether to make efforts to preserve the confidentiality" and removing "without limitation" before 'by cooperating with the Disclosing Party', making it more ambiguous.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts Rule of Professional Conduct 1.6(b) permits lawyers to reveal confidential information to the extent the lawyer reasonably believes necessary [paraphrased for clarity]. The change suggests counsel can disclose information that is merely 'advisable,' which is far broader than what the ethics rules allow. This conflicts with an attorney's duty to protect client confidences.

perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the original obligation to preserve confidentiality and introduces ambiguity. The original text mandated 'commercially reasonable efforts to preserve the confidentiality,' while the changed text only requires the 'Recipient considers whether to make efforts to preserve the confidentiality.' This significantly reduces the obligation. The Law Explanation mentions that the change suggests counsel can disclose information that is merely 'advisable,' which is far broader than what the ethics rules allow. This conflicts with an attorney's duty to protect client confidences, creating a contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 points to Massachusetts rules of professional conduct, reinforcing the importance of attorney conduct and ethical obligations, which the change potentially undermines.

perturbation.original_text: Following termination of discussions regarding a Possible Transaction, upon the request (which request shall be made within a reasonable time after such termination of discussions) of the Disclosing Party for any reason, Recipient will, at Recipient’s option, either deliver to the Disclosing Party or destroy (which destruction shall be certified in writing by an officer of Recipient) all Confidential Information furnished to Recipient or its Representatives by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party pursuant hereto and the portion of all other Confidential Information prepared by Recipient or its Representatives which contains Confidential Information furnished to Recipient or its Representatives by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party; provided, however, that Recipient and its Representatives (i) may each retain one copy of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information for recordkeeping purposes and for the purposes of defending its rights and obligations hereunder and (ii) will not be required to return or destroy -2- any computer or other electronic hardware or systems, to render any electronic data irrecoverable or to disable or otherwise modify any existing electronic data backup procedures.
perturbation.changed_text: Following termination of discussions regarding a Possible Transaction, upon the request (which request should be made within a generally reasonable time after such termination of discussions) of the Disclosing Party for any reason, Recipient might, at Recipient’s option, either deliver to the Disclosing Party or destroy (which destruction might be certified in writing by an officer of Recipient) all Confidential Information furnished to Recipient or its Representatives by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party pursuant hereto and the portion of all other Confidential Information prepared by Recipient or its Representatives which contains Confidential Information furnished to Recipient or its Representatives by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party; provided, however, that Recipient and its Representatives (i) may each retain one copy of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information for recordkeeping purposes and for the purposes of defending its rights and obligations hereunder and (ii) will not be required to return or destroy -2- any computer or other electronic hardware or systems, to render any electronic data irrecoverable or to disable or otherwise modify any existing electronic data backup procedures.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "request shall be made within a reasonable time" to "request should be made within a generally reasonable time", and "Recipient will" to "Recipient might", and "destruction shall be certified" to "destruction might be certified", the obligation to return or destroy confidential information is made less certain and harder to enforce, violating expectations. This can be seen as an unfair practice.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing inherent in Massachusetts contracts, which Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93A, Section 2 can be interpreted as applying to. By weakening the obligation to return or destroy confidential information and making actions optional, the altered text allows for actions that could be considered unfair or deceptive, potentially violating the law.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity regarding the recipient's obligation to return or destroy confidential information, and weakens the certainty of certified destruction. The original text states "Recipient will" deliver or destroy, and "destruction shall be certified". The changed text states "Recipient might" deliver or destroy, and "destruction might be certified", making these actions optional. This weakens the obligation. According to the Law Explanation, this change violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, potentially violating Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93A, Section 2. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, both quoting Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93A, Section 2(a), state that "Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful." By making the return or destruction of confidential information less certain, the revised contract introduces a risk of unfair practice, contradicting the law.

perturbation.original_text: It is further understood and agreed that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by the Recipient or any of its Representatives and that the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to equitable relief, including an injunction and specific performance, as a remedy for any such breach. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach by the Recipient of this Agreement but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: It is understood that money damages might not be a fully sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by the Recipient or any of its Representatives and that the Disclosing Party could potentially be entitled to equitable relief, perhaps including an injunction, as a possible remedy for any such breach. Such remedies might not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach by the Recipient of this Agreement but might be in addition to other remedies available at law or equity to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications replace "agreed that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy" with "understood that money damages might not be a fully sufficient remedy", and "Disclosing Party shall be entitled to equitable relief" with "Disclosing Party could potentially be entitled to equitable relief, perhaps including an injunction, as a possible remedy", and finally, "shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies" with "might not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies", undermining the assurance of specific performance or injunctive relief and leaving them open to debate.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified language casts doubt on the availability of equitable remedies, making them less certain and weakening the protections afforded to the disclosing party. This contradicts the principle that a party should have adequate remedies for breach of contract, and undermines the certainty needed for fair business practices in Massachusetts, in violation of the expectation under Chapter 93A.

Any persons entitled to bring such action may, if the use or employment of the unfair method of comp
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the assurance of equitable relief for the disclosing party in case of a breach, by changing 'shall be entitled' to 'could potentially be entitled' and introducing uncertainty with words like 'perhaps' and 'possible'. The original text makes specific performance or injunctive relief a definite remedy, while the changed text makes it a matter of chance. This creates an ambiguous legal obligation. According to Chapter 93A Section 11 (as evidenced in both snippets), equitable relief, including an injunction, should be available to a person who has suffered a loss because of unfair practices. The changed text undermines the availability of this equitable relief, thus contradicting the principle that parties should have adequate remedies for breach of contract.
